definition: A critical conceptual unit within the top term domain of the Exegesis. Mentions: 239.
interpretive_note: Plotinus (205–270 CE), the founder of Neoplatonism, provided the primary structural framework that PKD used to articulate the cosmology he experienced in 1974. Plotinus’s model of the cosmos describes a continuous emanation from the ineffable 'One', down through the 'Nous' (Divine Intellect) and the 'World Soul', finally descending into the material world. Dick used this emanationist model to explain how reality consists of descending levels of information and form. He heavily relied on Plotinus's concept that human souls are amphibians, living partly in the material world but retaining an eternal, unbroken link to the Divine Intellect. For Dick, anamnesis was the process of ascending this Neoplatonic ladder, turning one's gaze away from the confusing multiplicity of matter to perceive the unified, rational structure of the Nous.

One could assume, I guess, that the divine seed is in every one of us, but remains dormant (crypto morphosis); it is never fed, and this feeding comes to the pineal body via sunlight. So there is no implantation. I myself, I felt something (Firebright) was implanted. I had visions of Firebright's heavenly parents. That sounds like more that celestial food. Which do we have, a quickening, an awakening, or an implantation? The parables about the seeds sown.

Anyhow, this has been recast by God. The silence is broken. And I felt it that day when the first light (this year, 1975) up by the palm trees and the post office, the first springtime, must have struck my pineal body deep within me (Kathy: Santa Sophia as woman) alas, in the grass). It began to happen this spring, perhaps.

The Neoplatonist structure of Plotinus goes: The One, who sends his first emanatino Nous (Intellect) to construct the universe. We can rise up into the eternal world or reality, of forms, to apprehend Nous. The forms are alive; collectively, they comprise Nous. Below them is Soul.
